risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity. Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: Although minimization is not a classic simple random method, it is a systematic random allocation approach used to generate treatment assignments, and the text also reports stratification by baseline factors. This provides sufficient detail to judge the sequence generation as plausibly random/properly allocated rather than non-random.

Summary: The study reports that patients were "randomized 2:1" using "a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity," but it gives no explicit information on how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge. Comment: Because randomization method is described without allocation concealment details, the risk of allocation concealment bias is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme replacement therapy versus placebo" and "the control group received a volume matched placebo infusion." Comment: The study explicitly states that it was double-blind and used a volume-matched placebo infusion, which indicates participants and treating personnel were intended to be blinded. The main outcomes reported here (6-minute walk test, forced vital capacity, respiratory function, and cardiovascular measures) could in principle be influenced by knowledge of allocation, but the described double-blind placebo-controlled design makes performance bias unlikely.

Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial" and "All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm." Also, "All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessors for the reported cardiovascular outcomes were independent cardiologists blinded to allocation, and the outcomes (ECG/echocardiographic parameters) are objective measurements unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of treatment assignment.

Quote: "Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study." Comment: Attrition is small overall but clearly unbalanced between arms (14% vs 8%), a 6 percentage point difference. The paper also states that efficacy analyses used only completers: "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included in the efficacy analysis." This is a complete-case/per-protocol style approach rather than a robust missing-data method. Given the differential loss and use of completers-only analysis, missing outcome data could bias the result.
